candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be turned into a precise universality theorem: identify a minimal set of structural invariants under which very different micro-mechanisms provably generate the same power-law or scale-free regime, and characterize when substitutions among mechanisms are genuinely equivalent versus only superficially similar?

How can one rigorously distinguish true absence of intrinsic scale from merely hidden or weak cutoffs in finite, resource-constrained systems, and determine whether observed power laws are transient crossover phenomena, asymptotic properties, or statistical artifacts of limited data and model selection?

What exactly are the compensatory tradeoffs among amplification, replenishment, and constraint structure: if one load-bearing mechanism is weakened or removed, what alternative mechanisms can preserve cross-scale amplification, and is there a conservation-like relation or phase diagram separating power-law, winner-take-all, and thin-tailed regimes?
